meals: Take 400 micrograms of folic acid daily (best taken after meals with water) to prevent neural tube defects.
Consume 55–65 grams of high-quality protein daily from fish, eggs, milk, and meat.
Eat fresh vegetables and fruits to ensure 110–115 mg of Vitamin C per day.
Drink 200–400 ml of milk daily for calcium, and 1–1.5 liters of water throughout the day.
Get lecithin from egg yolk, soy, tofu, and black fungus to support fetal brain development.
Ensure adequate Vitamin B6 (about 2.4 mg/day from whole wheat cereal) to ease early nausea.
avoid: Avoid heavy meat-dominant diets; keep food light and easy to digest.
Avoid health supplements like ginseng and royal jelly, which may interfere with fetal development.
Avoid high-fat snacks such as French fries, chocolate, and fried foods.
Avoid street food due to poor hygiene and excess seasoning.
Avoid canned and heavily processed foods.
Avoid X-ray examinations; if necessary wear a lead apron to protect the abdomen.
Carefully wash all produce to remove pesticide residue.
Do not take any medication without doctor guidance; early pregnancy is especially sensitive.
events: Week 1–2: The precursor stage — egg and sperm still separate; no fertilization yet.
Week 2: Mature egg is released from the ovary; optimal fertilization window is 15–18 hours after ovulation.
Week 3: Egg and sperm meet to form a fertilized egg; new life begins.
Visit the hospital to confirm pregnancy by blood or urine HCG test as soon as menstruation is missed.
Record the last menstrual date to help doctors calculate the due date.
tips: Start treating yourself as a pregnant woman from now on; adjust habits and lifestyle.
Minimize radiation exposure: reduce Wi-Fi router proximity and excessive phone use.
Sleep before 11 PM and prioritize rest to support hormonal balance.
Early pregnancy is a high-risk period for miscarriage — avoid overexertion and heavy lifting.
Common early signs: missed period, fatigue, frequent urination, nausea, and breast tenderness.

meals: Continue folic acid supplementation at 400 micrograms daily.
Consume 55–65 grams of protein daily from diverse sources including fish, eggs, soy, and dairy.
Eat a variety of grains, vegetables, and fruits; aim for at least 150 grams of carbohydrates per day.
Drink at least 200–400 ml of milk and 1–1.5 liters of water daily.
avoid: Avoid dieting or restricting food intake; nutrient needs are beginning to increase.
Avoid hot tub baths and high-temperature exposure that may affect egg quality before conception.
events: Ovulation occurs around Week 2; track basal body temperature or use ovulation test strips to confirm.
Fertilization typically happens in the fallopian tube shortly after ovulation.
tips: Measure basal body temperature daily and record results to understand your cycle.
Maintain a healthy pre-conception weight — both underweight and overweight affect fertility.
Avoid alcohol, smoking, and recreational drugs entirely before and during pregnancy.

meals: Continue folic acid (400 mcg/day) and maintain regular varied meals.
Daily dietary reference for early pregnancy: 200–300g grains, 50–100g legumes, 200–250ml milk/yogurt, 500–600g vegetables and fruit, 25–50g eggs, 50–100g poultry/fish/meat, and 20g vegetable oil.
avoid: Avoid alcohol and smoking entirely — both severely harm embryo development.
Avoid medications not prescribed by a doctor, especially in the first 30 days of pregnancy.
events: Fertilized egg implants into the uterine lining around Week 3; slight implantation bleeding is normal.
HCG hormone begins to rise; urine pregnancy tests become detectable around this time.
tips: Begin adjusting your mindset for the role of motherhood; emotional stability is important for early development.
Avoid contact with household chemical cleaners, pesticides, and paint fumes.

meals: If nausea begins, eat small frequent meals and choose foods you find appealing rather than forcing specific options.
Take folic acid (400 mcg) daily without interruption through at least the first trimester.
Drink 200–400 ml of milk daily for calcium and protein.
avoid: Avoid sexual activity if there is a history of miscarriage or signs of threatened miscarriage.
Avoid household renovation work and exposure to paint fumes or chemical solvents.
Do not self-medicate, including topical medications; consult a doctor first.
events: Embryo is now the size of a poppy seed; the neural tube begins forming.
Visit hospital for formal pregnancy confirmation via blood HCG and progesterone test.
Schedule early prenatal hospital registration (建档) — most hospitals require this before Week 12.
tips: Avoid large doses of over-the-counter vitamins and supplements without medical advice.
Nausea, fatigue, breast tenderness, and frequent urination are normal early pregnancy symptoms.
Avoid using strong whitening or retinol cosmetics — some contain chemicals unsafe in pregnancy.

meals: Increase protein intake to 55–65g daily; include fish, eggs, dairy, and soy products.
Eat small, frequent meals to manage morning sickness; focus on easily digestible foods.
Continue folic acid (400 mcg/day) to support neural system development — this month is critical.
Eat iodine-rich foods such as seaweed and saltwater fish 2–3 times per week.
Eat walnuts in moderation (no more than 4 per day), mushrooms, and leafy greens for key nutrients.
avoid: Avoid pure vegetarian diet — insufficient protein and fat impair fetal brain development.
Do not take warming herbal supplements like ginseng, deer antler, or ejiao — they can worsen nausea and cause complications.
Avoid persimmons on an empty stomach — they can cause gallstones.
Do not take anti-nausea medications without doctor approval.
events: Week 5: Embryo's major organs begin forming — the heart starts beating.
HCG and progesterone blood test confirms healthy pregnancy levels.
Ultrasound around Week 6–7 confirms gestational sac location and rules out ectopic pregnancy.
tips: Avoid using contact lenses during pregnancy — hormonal changes can cause corneal swelling.
Use SPF15 sunscreen if going outdoors; avoid strong skincare actives.
Keep plastic bags and tissues handy at work to manage sudden nausea.
Nausea is worsened by fatigue — rest is a key remedy.

meals: Eat light, easily digestible foods; white rice porridge, steamed vegetables, and millet soup are ideal.
Drink plain boiled water as the best beverage — 1–1.5 liters daily.
Ensure zinc intake (about 10 mg/day) from seafood, animal liver, meat, fish, and legumes to support fetal brain development.
Eat soy products moderately — they provide excellent plant-based protein comparable to animal protein.
avoid: Avoid spinach in large quantities — its oxalic acid interferes with iron and calcium absorption.
Do not drink carbonated beverages — phosphate reacts with iron in the body causing iron deficiency.
Avoid canned or heavily processed foods.
events: Fetal heartbeat detectable by ultrasound around Week 6.
If vomiting is severe and you cannot keep any food down, see a doctor for evaluation.
tips: Shower (not bathing in hot tubs) with water temperature around 38–42°C to avoid overheating.
Avoid electric blankets — electromagnetic fields may harm the fetus.
Start sleeping on a firm but comfortable mattress to support your back.

meals: Keep meals light and varied; eat what you can tolerate rather than forcing specific healthy foods.
Add dairy and egg-based dishes for protein and calcium.
Avoid long-simmered bone broth — it is high in fat and purine despite being commonly thought nutritious.
avoid: Avoid spicy foods — they worsen morning sickness and may cause fetal discomfort.
Avoid eating raw fish, raw meat, and undercooked eggs — bacteria and parasites are harmful.
Do not use essential oils without medical consultation.
events: Embryo is now about 1 cm long; fingers and toes start forming.
Register at your hospital's obstetrics department and establish a prenatal health file.
tips: Practice deep breathing and simple relaxation techniques to manage pregnancy anxiety.
Keep the home well-ventilated; open windows periodically for fresh air.
Keep a pregnancy journal to document thoughts, feelings, and symptoms.

meals: Continue light, small-portion meals; morning sickness often peaks around Week 8–10.
Ensure 55–65g of daily protein from varied sources.
Eat fresh citrus fruits, strawberries, kiwi, and leafy greens for Vitamin C and folate.
avoid: Avoid excessive chocolate — it is high in caffeine and sugar.
Do not drink alcohol in any amount.
Avoid large doses of Vitamin A supplements — excess retinol is teratogenic.
events: HCG levels peak around Week 8–10, then begin to gradually decline.
Ultrasound confirms gestational sac, fetal pole, and heartbeat.
Embryo is about 1.5 cm; all major organs are present in rudimentary form.
tips: Morning sickness is at its worst — try eating dry crackers before getting out of bed.
Avoid over-exercising; gentle walks are the safest physical activity.
Discuss workplace rights and pregnancy accommodations with your employer.

meals: Eat millet, corn, and red sweet potato as part of mixed grain staples for carbohydrates and magnesium.
Consume 310 mg of magnesium per day from green vegetables, nuts, pumpkin, sunflower seeds, and whole grains.
Include DHA-rich foods: salmon, sardines, tuna, shrimp, chicken, and walnuts.
Ensure 770 mcg of Vitamin A daily from animal liver, egg yolk, and lean meats.
Take 14 mg Vitamin E daily naturally from vegetable oils, nuts, and sunflower seeds.
avoid: Avoid cured meats, smoked fish, and sausages — they contain nitrosamines that can cause fetal abnormalities.
Avoid raw or undercooked fish, meat, and eggs.
Avoid eating large amounts of watermelon — it is cold and diuretic.
Do not replace full meals with fruit — fruit lacks sufficient protein and fat for pregnancy.
events: First official prenatal checkup (1st prenatal visit) at weeks 11–12 is approaching — book your appointment.
NT ultrasound screening is best performed at weeks 11–14 to assess chromosomal abnormality risk.
Fetus is now the size of a strawberry; fingers and toes are clearly formed.
tips: Learn proper posture for housework — avoid bending over or heavy lifting.
Avoid wearing tight clothing; begin transitioning to maternity wear.
Avoid contact with cats and their litter boxes — risk of toxoplasmosis.

meals: Focus on food quality and variety over quantity.
Drink yogurt daily for gut health and calcium.
Eat tofu frequently — soy lecithin promotes fetal brain development.
avoid: Avoid perfumed or whitening cosmetics — many contain chemical compounds unsafe in pregnancy.
Avoid prolonged noise exposure — it can raise blood pressure.
events: NT scan recommended between weeks 11–14; values under 3mm are considered normal.
Fetal size reaches about 3–4 cm; all major organs are fully formed in rudimentary form.
tips: Change to maternity underwear for better pelvic support.
Watch uplifting films or read positive books — emotional wellbeing directly affects fetal environment.
Start gentle foot exercises to reduce leg swelling.

meals: Maintain a balanced diet with carbohydrates, protein, and healthy fats; variety is more important than quantity.
Increase fiber intake from whole grains, oats, corn, sweet potato, and vegetables to prevent constipation.
Sip water throughout the day rather than drinking large amounts at once.
avoid: Avoid very spicy foods — chili and hot spices cause fetal discomfort and worsen constipation.
Avoid long-simmered pork bone soup — it is high in fat with limited nutritional benefit.
events: First prenatal checkup (1st prenatal visit) at weeks 11–12; includes blood tests, urine, blood pressure, weight, and dating ultrasound.
NT screening performed this week — normal NT value is under 3mm.
Build the hospital prenatal card (建档) within Week 12.
tips: Use a hospital pregnancy health booklet to track all checkups.
Avoid prolonged sitting and contact sports.
If morning sickness has eased, gradually resume a fuller, more varied diet.

meals: Morning sickness typically fades by Week 12; gradually return to a full, balanced diet.
Begin adding more calcium-rich foods: milk, shrimp, sesame, and leafy greens.
Continue folic acid and maintain a balanced plate across all food groups.
avoid: Avoid excessive fruit as a meal replacement — it lacks key macronutrients.
Do not skip meals in an attempt to control weight gain.
events: Week 12 marks the end of the first trimester — miscarriage risk drops significantly.
First prenatal appointment includes full blood panel, thyroid check, rubella immunity, and dating ultrasound.
First prenatal visit checklist: blood type, CBC, hepatitis B, syphilis, HIV, urine analysis, and fetal measurement.
tips: Begin gentle prenatal yoga or walking if approved by your doctor.
High-risk pregnancy evaluation — discuss any risk factors openly with your OB.
Avoid whitening cosmetics — some ingredients are unsafe during pregnancy.

meals: Increase daily protein by 15g above pre-pregnancy levels — add 50–100g of fish, poultry, eggs, or lean meat.
Drink at least 250ml milk daily; supplement with calcium pills if dairy is insufficient.
Iron intake increases to 25 mg/day — eat red meat, liver, and pair with Vitamin C to improve absorption.
Daily dietary reference for weeks 13–28: 250–300g grains, 50–100g eggs, 100–150g meat/poultry/fish, 50g liver 1–2x/week, 50–100g legumes, 500g vegetables, 200g fruit, 20g oil.
avoid: Avoid hotpot — raw or briefly cooked meat may contain parasites not killed by brief cooking.
Avoid unripe green tomatoes — they contain toxic solanine.
Avoid excessive caloric intake — weight gain accelerates this month; limit sugary foods, fried foods, and excess fruit.
events: Second trimester begins — fetal growth accelerates significantly.
Baby's limbs are fully proportioned; fetus begins making active movement.
Down's syndrome prenatal screening is ideally performed between weeks 15–20.
tips: Begin regular prenatal checkups every 4 weeks from this month.
Distribute calories: breakfast 30%, lunch 40%, dinner 30%; eat healthy snacks between meals.
Appetite typically improves this month — use this window to regain nutritional balance.

meals: Eat cherries in moderate amounts — they are rich in iron and help prevent anemia.
Eat winter melon regularly to reduce edema.
Consume beta-carotene from carrots, pumpkin, and sweet potato to support fetal bone development.
Supplement iron through both diet and supplements regardless of anemia status.
avoid: Avoid drinking unboiled soy milk — raw soy contains substances harmful to digestion.
Avoid excessive fruit — it is high in sugar and can raise gestational diabetes risk.
Do not take calcium supplements alongside oxalate-rich foods like spinach.
events: Down syndrome screening blood test recommended between weeks 15–20; best at weeks 16–18.
Amniocentesis may be recommended for mothers over 35 — weeks 16–20 is ideal.
Baby's teeth buds begin forming at Week 14.
tips: Calcium supplements should be spread throughout the day and not taken all at once.
Safe exercise this month: swimming, prenatal yoga, and walking.
Prevent nutritional excess — oversized babies complicate labor.

meals: Focus on distributing calorie intake evenly across three meals.
Maintain iron and folate supplementation.
Increase legume intake — soy, black beans, lentils — for plant protein and folate.
avoid: Avoid excessive processed snacks with artificial flavors and preservatives.
Do not drink carbonated beverages.
events: Down's syndrome screening blood test should ideally be performed this week if not yet done.
Fetus can now make sucking and swallowing motions; first fetal movements may be felt soon.
tips: Maternity bras with good support are recommended as breasts continue to enlarge.
Sleep on your left side to improve blood flow to the placenta.
Begin researching childbirth classes at your hospital.

meals: Consume 230 mcg of iodine per day from iodized salt, seaweed, kelp, shrimp, and marine fish — the baby's thyroid becomes active this month.
60g fat per day from peanuts, sesame, egg yolk, animal liver, and plant oils is sufficient.
Maintain 1000 mg calcium daily: 500ml milk plus calcium from other dietary sources.
avoid: Avoid hotpot and rare or undercooked meats.
Avoid excessive caloric intake to prevent rapid weight gain.
events: Down syndrome screening blood test should be completed by Week 16–18.
Amniocentesis for mothers over 35 performed at weeks 16–20.
First fetal movements (胎动) are often felt for the first time around weeks 16–18.
tips: Early fetal movements feel like light fluttering or bubbles — distinguish them from digestion.
Begin tracking fetal movements once clearly felt; record 3 times daily.
Talk to your baby and play gentle music as fetal education begins.

meals: Focus on 5 key nutrients: Vitamin C (115 mg/day), Vitamin D (10 mcg/day), selenium (65 mcg/day), calcium (1000 mg/day), and iron (25 mg/day).
Eat pig liver 2–3 times per week in small portions (25g each) for iron and Vitamin A.
Snack on red dates, chestnuts, peanuts, and sunflower seeds between meals.
Eat selenium-rich foods: pork kidney, fish, shrimp, lamb, beef, sesame, and yam.
avoid: Avoid very cold foods and drinks — the fetus is now sensitive to temperature stimuli.
Avoid preserved eggs — they may contain lead.
Avoid hot spices like star anise, pepper, cinnamon, and five-spice powder — they cause constipation.
Avoid raw oysters and shellfish — risk of bacterial or viral infection.
events: Amniocentesis is most effective at weeks 16–20 — amniotic fluid volume is ideal.
Fetal movements become more regular and recognizable around Week 17–18.
Fetal bone development accelerates — skeleton is rapidly lengthening.
tips: Get 1–2 hours of outdoor sunlight daily to support natural Vitamin D synthesis.
Eat 5–6 small meals a day to manage growing nutritional demands without overloading digestion.
Count fetal movements: 3–5 times per hour is typical; fewer than 10 times in 12 hours warrants a doctor visit.

meals: Add Vitamin C-rich foods: bok choy, rapeseed greens, broccoli, bitter melon, grapefruit, oranges, lemon, strawberries, and kiwi.
Ensure 1000 mg calcium per day from dairy, fish, shrimp, egg yolk, seaweed, and sesame.
Eat poultry or lean meat liver 1–2x per week (25g) to meet iron goals (25 mg/day).
avoid: Avoid preserved eggs and canned foods that may contain lead.
Avoid excessive fruit — high sugar content raises gestational diabetes risk.
events: The baby can now hear sounds — begin music-based fetal education.
Fetal movements should be clearly felt by Week 18–20.
tips: Play soft classical music close to the abdomen for 15–30 minutes daily as fetal education.
Sleep on your left side with a pillow between your knees for comfort.
Avoid tight belts or pants that press on the belly.

meals: Drink fresh lemon juice for Vitamin C and to improve iron absorption.
Eat carrot and tomato juice for beta-carotene and antioxidants.
Eat varied grains at each meal to ensure complete B-vitamin intake.
avoid: Avoid preserved eggs.
Avoid very cold or iced foods and drinks.
events: Major morphology ultrasound (大排畸B超) is typically scheduled between weeks 20–24 — book the appointment.
Baby is about 15 cm and 200g; can make facial expressions and swallow amniotic fluid.
tips: Prepare maternity clothing with non-restrictive waistbands.
Begin thinking about birth plan preferences: natural birth vs. C-section, pain management options.
Continue daily gentle walking and stretching.

meals: Continue well-balanced meals with adequate protein, iron, calcium, and vitamins at the pregnancy midpoint.
Eat seasonal fruit for natural vitamins and hydration.
Continue eating fish 2–3 times per week as the primary DHA source.
avoid: Avoid excessive salt — sodium causes water retention and raises blood pressure.
Avoid preserved and fermented products high in sodium.
events: Anatomy scan (大排畸检查) performed around weeks 20–24 — checks all major fetal organs.
Fetal movement should be counted daily; normal range is 30–100 times per 12 hours.
Baby is about 20 cm and 300g; can hear, taste, and respond to touch.
tips: Record fetal movement 3 times daily (after meals) for 1 hour each session.
Attend prenatal education classes if available at your hospital.
Practice kegel exercises to strengthen pelvic floor muscles.

meals: Eat fiber-rich vegetables (celery, leek, bok choy), oats, corn, millet, and whole grains (20–30g fiber/day) to prevent constipation.
Eat low-fat yogurt regularly — it aids digestion and provides calcium.
Add 15g of extra high-quality protein daily (for second trimester); 200ml milk + 10g lean meat or 1 egg provides this.
Eat brain-supporting fats from fish, nuts, and corn oil rich in unsaturated fatty acids.
Eat foods rich in Vitamin B12: pork liver, kidney, beef, chicken, clams, eggs, and milk.
avoid: Avoid eating too fast — impairs digestion and nutrient absorption.
Avoid carbonated drinks — phosphate depletes iron.
Avoid food with artificial colors, preservatives, and nitrates — they increase risk of complications.
Avoid dissolving nutrition powders in hot water — heat destroys most nutrients.
events: 3D ultrasound for fetal anomaly screening ideally performed at weeks 24–28.
4D ultrasound can be done at weeks 26–30 for detailed anatomical imaging.
Fetus has rapid brain growth — a key period for intellectual development.
tips: Control salt intake — cook at home and reduce added salt to prevent edema.
Avoid sugary foods (cakes, chocolate, sweetened drinks) to reduce gestational diabetes risk.
Take a gentle walk after dinner to improve digestion and blood circulation.

meals: Increase iron to 25 mg/day from animal liver and red meat; pair with Vitamin C for better absorption.
Eat Vitamin B12-rich foods: pork liver, pork kidney, beef, chicken, tuna, shrimp, eggs, milk, and cheese.
Eat orange-colored foods (carrots, pumpkin, sweet potato) for beta-carotene and Vitamin A.
avoid: Avoid processed high-sodium snacks.
Limit caffeine — reduce coffee, black tea, and green tea intake.
events: The baby is about 28 cm and 450g; eyes are formed and can perceive light.
Fetal hearing is fully developed — music and voice stimulation are particularly effective now.
tips: Read aloud or play music daily as part of fetal education.
Dress in loose, layered clothing to accommodate body temperature changes.
Begin researching baby essentials: crib, stroller, and clothing.

meals: Continue high-protein, high-fiber diet with moderate carbohydrates.
Eat fish or shellfish 2–3 times a week as primary DHA and iodine sources.
Drink yogurt after meals (30 minutes to 2 hours post-meal) — not on an empty stomach.
avoid: Do not skip a meal and then overeat at the next — irregular eating converts excess calories to fat.
Do not drink yogurt on an empty stomach — stomach acid destroys the beneficial bacteria.
events: Baby is about 30 cm; skin is translucent; beginning to store fat under the skin.
Continue monitoring blood pressure and urine protein at prenatal visits.
tips: Avoid prolonged standing or sitting; elevate feet when resting to reduce swelling.
Apply moisturizer or stretch mark oil to abdomen, thighs, and breasts daily.
Begin practicing relaxation breathing techniques for labor preparation.

meals: Ensure adequate nutrition to support full fetal organ development — do not under-eat.
Maintain daily iron intake through diverse food sources.
Eat 500g fresh vegetables and 200g fruit daily.
avoid: Avoid eating too little due to fear of weight gain — the baby needs full nutritional support.
Avoid very salty restaurant meals.
events: Full anatomy scan (大排畸超声) should be completed by Week 24 — checks brain, heart, spine, kidneys, lips, and limbs.
Glucose challenge test (GCT) for gestational diabetes screening may begin this month.
Baby's lungs begin practicing breathing motions.
tips: Monitor blood pressure regularly — pregnancy-induced hypertension is most common after Week 20.
Begin preparing a hospital bag for delivery.
Sleep on left side; use pillows between knees for comfort.

meals: Focus on B-vitamin complex: Vitamin B1 from wheat flour, oats, soybeans, millet, pork; B2 from dairy, liver, eggs, eggplant, fish, celery, and oranges; B6 from liver, soybeans, oats, eggs, walnuts, and peanuts.
Include lecithin-rich foods: egg yolk, soybeans, corn oil, sunflower oil, animal liver, eel, and corn — supports fetal brain development.
Aim for 70–85g protein daily; split evenly between animal and plant sources.
Drink 6–8 cups (1200 ml) of water daily to prevent dehydration.
Eat nuts (walnuts, sesame, peanuts) and whole grains daily for brain-supporting B-vitamins and healthy fats.
avoid: Avoid sugary foods — high blood pressure risk increases this month.
Avoid spicy, irritating foods (mustard, chili, curry) — they cause fetal discomfort.
Avoid lychee in excess — it is a warming fruit that raises blood sugar and causes heat symptoms.
Avoid salty foods — they worsen edema.
events: 75g glucose tolerance test (OGTT) to screen for gestational diabetes — performed at weeks 24–28.
Baby's brain enters a rapid growth phase; third trimester begins soon.
Fetus is about 35 cm and 700g; can open eyes and respond to light.
tips: Gestational diabetes screening (OGTT): fast for 12 hours the night before; drink glucose solution slowly and walk afterward.
Monitor blood pressure at home; visit doctor if readings exceed 140/90 mmHg.
Reduce salt in cooking; avoid adding extra salt at the table.

meals: Eat brain-nourishing foods high in lecithin and omega-3: egg yolk, soybeans, deep-sea fish, and walnuts.
Avoid excessive red meat; substitute with fish for a healthier fat profile.
avoid: Avoid artificial sweeteners and foods with artificial flavors.
Do not ignore food labels — avoid foods high in additives.
events: OGTT glucose tolerance test performed between weeks 24–28.
4D color ultrasound can be scheduled between weeks 26–30 for detailed fetal imaging.
Baby is about 36 cm and 900g; significant fat is now being stored under skin.
tips: If experiencing heartburn, eat smaller meals and avoid lying down immediately after eating.
Prop your head up slightly when sleeping if heartburn is severe.
Practice pelvic tilts and gentle prenatal stretches daily.

meals: Continue high-protein diet: 70–85g/day from fish, chicken, eggs, and soy.
Drink 1200ml water per day; reduce if edema is severe.
Eat corn, sesame, and sunflower oil for essential fatty acids and brain support.
avoid: Avoid too much red meat and animal fat.
Limit refined sugar intake — gestational diabetes risk is highest this trimester.
events: OGTT glucose tolerance test if not yet done.
Baby is over 37 cm and 1000g; hearing is fully functional.
Normal fetal movement: at least 10 times in 2 hours.
tips: Begin learning about signs of preterm labor: regular contractions before Week 37.
Avoid prolonged standing — this worsens varicose veins and edema.
Partner involvement: encourage the father to talk to the baby and attend prenatal visits.

meals: Continue iron supplementation (25–29 mg/day) from red meat, liver, pork blood, and lean beef.
Include alpha-linolenic acid foods: flaxseed oil, walnut oil, perilla oil, walnuts, pine nuts, and almonds for DHA synthesis.
Limit nuts to no more than 50g per day despite their benefits.
avoid: Avoid excessive nuts — they are calorie-dense and cause digestive upset in excess.
Avoid moon cakes and similar high-sugar, high-fat pastries.
events: Third trimester begins — prenatal visit frequency increases to every 2 weeks.
Baby is about 38 cm and 1100g; eyelids open and close; practices breathing movements.
Blood pressure monitoring becomes more critical — preeclampsia risk increases.
tips: Switch prenatal visits from monthly to every 2 weeks.
Blood pressure screening for gestational hypertension is a priority this trimester.
Begin home fetal movement monitoring: count 3x daily for 1 hour each after meals.

meals: Increase nutrition by 20–40% compared to pre-pregnancy; eat 5–6 small meals daily.
Eat 200–350g grains, 80–85g protein, 60g fat, 200g fruit, 500–750g vegetables, and 250–500ml milk per day.
Use corn oil, sesame oil, sunflower oil, and walnut oil to obtain alpha-linolenic acid for DHA.
Iron intake: 29 mg/day from pork liver, pork blood, and lean beef.
Supplement copper (0.9 mg/day) from mandarins, apples, chestnuts, sesame, mushrooms, and seafood.
avoid: Avoid high-calorie foods to prevent excessive weight gain — limit to 250–500g per week.
Avoid raw salad greens — lightly blanch vegetables before eating.
Do not consume excessive nuts — limit to 50g per day.
events: Prenatal visits now every 2 weeks; includes blood pressure, urine protein, and fetal size measurement.
Gestational hypertension screening is a priority — monitor BP for values above 140/90.
Baby is rapidly gaining weight: about 40 cm and 1300g.
tips: If weekly weight gain exceeds 550g, reduce carbohydrate intake and consult your doctor.
Watch for signs of preeclampsia: swelling, protein in urine, severe headache, blurred vision.
Sleep on the left side to reduce pressure on the vena cava and improve placental blood flow.

meals: Continue high-protein diet (80–85g/day) with fish, chicken, eggs, and soy.
Ensure daily alpha-linolenic acid from flaxseed, walnut, and perilla oils for fetal DHA production.
Eat easily digestible soups and stews.
avoid: Avoid raw salads and undercooked vegetables.
Avoid overeating at one sitting — the stomach has less room due to uterine size.
events: Baby is now in a head-down position in most cases; doctor checks fetal position.
Kick count monitoring continues — report any significant decrease immediately.
Baby is about 43 cm and 1500g.
tips: Wear a maternity support belt if experiencing lower back pain.
Begin assembling your hospital delivery bag.
Practice relaxation techniques and prenatal breathing exercises daily.

meals: Maintain balanced nutrition with emphasis on protein and minerals.
Eat soup-based meals to ease swallowing and digestion as stomach room decreases.
Continue drinking adequate water to prevent constipation and support kidney function.
avoid: Avoid heavy, oily meals that worsen heartburn and indigestion.
Avoid moon cakes and similar high-fat, high-sugar pastries.
Avoid large portions of liquid at once — sip throughout the day.
events: Continue bi-weekly prenatal checkups; includes fetal monitoring and blood pressure check.
Baby is about 43 cm and 1700g; brain development is rapid.
Check fetal position — breech babies may be recommended for specific positional exercises.
tips: Avoid prolonged standing — elevate feet regularly to reduce edema.
Do not sleep on your back — this compresses major blood vessels.
If heartburn is severe, prop up the head of your bed by a few inches.

meals: Continue iron intake (29 mg/day) and calcium (1000 mg/day).
Eat high-quality protein at every meal: eggs, fish, milk, and chicken.
Eat sesame, pumpkin seeds, and walnuts as snacks for essential minerals.
avoid: Avoid high-heat, high-calorie fried foods.
Avoid large portions of liquid at once.
events: Baby is approximately 45 cm and 2000g — entering the fat-storage phase.
Gestational hypertension monitoring intensifies — blood pressure checked at every visit.
Third trimester growth ultrasound may be performed around Week 32.
tips: Begin learning about the stages of labor: early labor, active labor, pushing, and delivery.
Keep an emergency contact list and know how to reach your OB quickly.
Practice pelvic rocking and breathing exercises for labor preparation.

meals: Eat fiber-rich foods — celery, leek, bok choy, oats, corn, millet, and whole wheat bread — for constipation relief.
Eat foods rich in Vitamin B2 (1.5 mg/day): animal liver, eggs, milk, and soy products.
Calcium 1000mg+ daily: 2 cups of milk alone is insufficient — supplement with shrimp, kelp, seaweed, black fungus, and tofu.
Copper (0.9 mg/day): animal liver, legumes, seafood, green vegetables, and fruit.
avoid: Avoid smoked and grilled foods — they contain carcinogenic compounds.
Avoid chips and potato crisps — high in oil and sugar; may raise blood pressure.
Do not drink yogurt on an empty stomach — beneficial bacteria are destroyed by stomach acid.
Avoid irregular meal timing — overeating at one sitting converts excess calories to fat.
events: Prenatal checkups twice monthly; includes fetal size, position, amniotic fluid, and umbilical cord assessment.
Umbilical cord around the neck (脐带绕颈) is common — most babies self-resolve; count kicks and monitor.
Baby is about 47 cm and 2200g; lungs are nearly mature.
tips: Sleep on the left side; use pillows for support.
Count fetal movement twice daily using a home fetal monitor if available.
Do not attempt to manually turn a breech baby — follow doctor's advice only.

meals: Vitamin K (80 mcg/day) is critical now: eat cauliflower, bok choy, spinach, bamboo shoots, broccoli, purple cabbage, cheese, liver, and cereal grains.
Continue calcium and iron supplementation.
Eat small, frequent meals — 5–6 times daily — as stomach space decreases.
avoid: Avoid smoked or grilled meats.
Avoid eating too quickly or too much at once.
events: Baby is about 48 cm and 2300g; head begins to engage toward the pelvis.
Begin discussing delivery plan with doctor — vaginal birth vs. C-section considerations.
Baby's immune antibodies are being transferred through the placenta.
tips: Install baby safety equipment at home: crib rails, outlet covers.
Practice breathing and pushing techniques from birth classes.
Prepare postpartum care supplies for after delivery.

meals: Eat light, easily digestible meals — avoid rich, heavy food as labor approaches.
Eat Vitamin K-rich vegetables to reduce postpartum bleeding risk.
Continue iron (29 mg/day) to prepare for blood loss during delivery.
avoid: Avoid eating too much in anticipation of labor — a very large baby complicates delivery.
Avoid long-cooked silver ear (银耳) soup left overnight — it can produce potentially harmful compounds.
events: Baby is about 50 cm and 2700g — considered near-full-term.
Prenatal visit frequency increases to weekly from Week 36.
Baby's fine lanugo hair and vernix caseosa begin to shed.
tips: Pack your hospital bag if not done already.
Wash and prepare all newborn clothing and bedding.
Avoid taking long drives or traveling far from home.

meals: Eat light, easily digestible foods — rice porridge, steamed vegetables, and fish are ideal.
Continue Vitamin B1 supplementation from whole grains and legumes to ensure efficient uterine contractions.
Stay well-hydrated but sip water gradually rather than in large amounts.
avoid: Avoid eating excessively — a very large baby complicates vaginal delivery.
Avoid restaurants and takeout — harder to control salt and oil content.
events: Weekly prenatal checkups begin at Week 36.
Internal cervical exam may begin — assessment of cervical readiness for labor.
Baby is fully formed and practicing breathing — typically weighs 2500–3000g.
tips: Stay close to home and keep emergency contact numbers accessible.
Know the signs of true labor: regular contractions every 5 minutes, water breaking, and bloody show.
Practice left-side sleeping and count kicks 3x daily.

meals: Eat light, nutritious foods for labor energy: broccoli, purple cabbage, cantaloupe, wheat bread, whole grains, and legumes for Vitamin K.
Eat Vitamin B12-rich foods (3.1 mcg/day): 2 servings of meat plus 1 cup of milk and 1 egg daily to support baby's myelin development.
Eat Vitamin K-rich foods (80 mcg/day): egg yolk, cheese, seaweed, lotus root, spinach, bok choy, cauliflower, and soybeans.
Iron (29 mg/day) and zinc (9.5 mg/day) from pork liver, lean pork, fish, oysters, soybeans, mung beans, peanuts, and walnuts.
avoid: Avoid oily, greasy foods — they cause bloating and reduce comfort near delivery.
Avoid large amounts of sweets before labor — they can interfere with uterine contractions.
Do not eat overnight silver ear soup — it degrades and may produce harmful compounds.
Limit eating out — home cooking allows better control of salt and oil.
events: Baby is considered full-term at Week 37 — about 3000g and 50 cm.
Weekly prenatal checkups; includes cervical dilation check and fetal position confirmation.
Fetal head engages into the pelvis — breathing becomes easier but pelvic pressure increases.
tips: Know the three signs of labor: regular contractions, bloody mucus show, and water breaking.
Go to the hospital immediately if contractions are 5 minutes apart or water breaks.
Rest and conserve energy for labor.
Practice relaxation breathing daily in preparation for delivery.

meals: Continue light, energy-rich meals with Vitamin B1, B12, Vitamin K, iron, and zinc.
Eat easily digestible foods to keep energy available for labor.
Drink adequate water but avoid large amounts at once near delivery.
avoid: Avoid heavy, fatty meals that cause indigestion and discomfort.
Avoid excessive sweets.
events: Baby's lanugo is shedding; skin is smooth and increasingly plump.
Amniotic fluid index (AFI) is checked: normal is 5–18 cm; below 5 requires immediate attention.
Fetal position confirmed — breech position at this stage likely requires C-section.
tips: Count fetal movements 3x per day; report any significant reduction immediately.
Pack final items in hospital bag; have it ready by the door.
Arrange childcare or pet care for the delivery period.

meals: Continue nutritious light meals to store energy for delivery.
Eat Vitamin B1-rich foods (whole grains, legumes, nuts, green leafy vegetables) to support uterine contractions.
Eat zinc-rich foods: pork liver, pork belly, lean meat, fish, and oysters.
avoid: Avoid eating large meals before labor — a full stomach makes labor and delivery uncomfortable.
Avoid iced or very cold drinks.
events: Baby is about 51 cm and 3200g; lung maturation continues.
Braxton Hicks contractions increase — they are irregular and do not intensify over time.
Cervical ripening may be evident during internal examination.
tips: Distinguish Braxton Hicks (irregular, don't intensify) from true labor (regular, intensify, persist).
Walk gently daily to encourage the baby to descend into the pelvis.
Discuss birth preferences with your support partner and birth team.

meals: Eat light, easily digestible foods — thin porridge or light soup during early labor is acceptable.
Eat Vitamin B1 and K rich foods to support uterine function during labor.
Avoid large meals once contractions begin — small sips of clear fluid are safer.
avoid: Avoid large, heavy meals near the time of delivery.
Avoid excessive sweets even if appetite spikes — energy should be conserved for labor.
Avoid dining out — control salt and oil at home.
events: Due date — only about 5% of babies are born on the exact due date.
If baby has not arrived by Week 40, weekly monitoring (NST and ultrasound) begins.
Signs of labor: regular contractions every 5 minutes lasting 30–60 seconds, bloody show, and water breaking.
Hospital admission is recommended when contractions are 5 minutes apart or when waters break.
tips: Stay calm — labor can begin anytime in the next 1–2 weeks even past the due date.
Keep your phone charged and hospital route planned.
Rest as much as possible to conserve energy for the birthing process.
After delivery, immediate skin-to-skin contact with the newborn promotes bonding and initiates breastfeeding.
